sources.full: Phebe (Grigson) Russell: third wife of Rev. John Russell, widow of Rev. John Whiting; d. 19 Sep 1730, New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of the phantom ship's lost merchant
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe's father, Thomas Gregson of New Haven, sailed in the colony's famous “phantom ship” that vanished at sea around 1647. The ghostly vessel later said to appear in the harbor became New England legend. Phebe carried that haunted lineage into two marriages.
Widow of Rev. John Whiting of Hartford, Phebe married Rev. John Russell of Hadley in 1692. She survived him as well, then removed to New Haven. She died there in 1730, near ninety.

sources.full: Phebe (Grigson) Russell: third wife of Rev. John Russell, widow of Rev. John Whiting; d. 19 Sep 1730, New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of the phantom ship's lost merchant
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe's father, Thomas Gregson of New Haven, sailed in the colony's famous “phantom ship” that vanished at sea around 1647. The ghostly vessel later said to appear in the harbor became New England legend. Phebe carried that haunted lineage into two marriages.
Widow of Rev. John Whiting of Hartford, Phebe married Rev. John Russell of Hadley in 1692. She survived him as well, then removed to New Haven. She died there in 1730, near ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: His church split; he founded another
narrative_blocks.body: The half-way covenant fight broke Hartford’s First Church in 1669, and thirty-two members walked out. Whiting petitioned the Assembly “for a distinct walking in Congregational Church Order,” won, and was ordained pastor of the new Second Church — which celebrated its 350th anniversary in 2020.

narrative_blocks.header: His bride’s father sailed the phantom ship
narrative_blocks.body: His second wife Phebe was the daughter of Thomas Gregson of New Haven, lost at sea in 1646 aboard the famous “phantom ship.” Phebe outlived Whiting by forty-one years, remarrying Rev. John Russell of Hadley.

His grandfather Major William Whiting was one of Hartford's founding settlers. His father Rev. John Whiting was the 4th Minister of Hartford. He became Hon. Col. Joseph Whiting of New Haven — and his children's double marriage to the Ingersolls would eventually connect this Hartford founding family line to Jared Ingersoll Jr., signer of the U.S. Constitution.

cross_connections.display_label: Their children made a double sibling marriage — son×daughter, daughter×son.
Their children made a double sibling marriage — son×daughter, daughter×son.

burial.plot_notes: Memorialized at bottom of shared stone with husband Jared in Center Church Crypt

narrative_blocks.header: The Ingersoll and Whiting families exchanged siblings
narrative_blocks.body: She married John Whiting; her brother Jared Ingersoll Sr. married John's sister Hannah Whiting. Two Ingersoll siblings and two Whiting siblings, cross-married in the same generation — the families of New Haven thoroughly interlaced.

narrative_blocks.header: The Ingersoll and Whiting families exchanged siblings
narrative_blocks.body: She married John Whiting; her brother Jared Ingersoll Sr. married John's sister Hannah Whiting. Two Ingersoll siblings and two Whiting siblings, cross-married in the same generation — the families of New Haven thoroughly interlaced.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Hon. Col. Joseph Whiting
narrative_blocks.body: His father Hon. Col. Joseph Whiting was a grandson of Major William Whiting, one of Hartford's founders, and son of Rev. John Whiting, the 4th Minister of Hartford. His sister Hannah Whiting married Jared Ingersoll Sr., the Connecticut Stamp Master whose son Jared Jr. signed the Constitution.

sources.full: Descendants of the Rev. John Russell of Cambridge, Wethersfield, and Hadley
Primary sketch of Rev. John Russell of Hadley: Harvard 1645, Wethersfield to Hadley, sheltering the regicides, 1693 estate inventory (incl. three enslaved people), Hopkins School fight.

notable.notable_blurb: Puritan minister; the Angel of Hadley

narrative_blocks.header: The minister who hid the king’s killers
narrative_blocks.body: For over a decade Russell secretly sheltered Edward Whalley and William Goffe — two of the judges who had condemned Charles I to death — beneath his Hadley parsonage while royal agents hunted them across the colonies. A single misstep meant death for him and his household. The historian George Sheldon later called him the “Guardian Angel of Hadley.”

narrative_blocks.header: An angel rallied Hadley, then vanished
narrative_blocks.body: During a King Philip’s War raid, an unknown gray-haired stranger appeared, took command of the panicked townspeople, drove off the attackers, and disappeared — leaving them convinced an angel had saved them. The deliverer was almost certainly the fugitive general Goffe, slipping from Russell’s house and back. The episode became New England’s enduring “Angel of Hadley” legend.

narrative_blocks.header: He led his flock out of Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: When a bitter dispute over baptism and church membership split the Wethersfield congregation, Russell drew up a covenant and gathered the dissenting families. In 1659 they agreed to leave the jurisdiction of Connecticut for Massachusetts. They crossed the wilderness and founded the town of Hadley on a bend of the Connecticut River.

narrative_blocks.header: Bones found beneath the parsonage cellar
narrative_blocks.body: When Russell’s house was pulled down near the end of the eighteenth century, workers lifting a cellar slab uncovered large human remains. Local tradition held them to be one of the regicides, secretly buried in the minister’s own foundation. A tooth from the find was kept by a witness for decades afterward.

narrative_blocks.header: Three enslaved people in his estate
narrative_blocks.body: The inventory of Russell’s estate, taken at Hadley in January 1693, listed an enslaved man, woman, and child together valued at sixty pounds. They are recorded among the household goods, a stark line in the ledger of a celebrated minister.

cross_connections.display_label: led the dissenting party Russell joined toward Hadley
